meta.title: HOMA-IR Calculator — Insulin Resistance | Health Calculators
meta.description: Calculate HOMA-IR from fasting glucose and fasting insulin. Formula, risk bands (normal, mild, insulin resistance, severe) and clinical context.

homaIr.title: HOMA-IR — Insulin Resistance Calculator
homaIr.description: Enter fasting blood glucose and fasting insulin. The calculator returns the HOMA-IR index and classifies it into four clinical risk bands.
homaIr.formulaWhy: The formula multiplies fasting insulin and fasting glucose. Higher products mean more insulin is needed to keep glucose in range — the signature of insulin resistance.
homaIr.clinicalNote: HOMA-IR is an estimate and does not replace an oral glucose tolerance test or a medical diagnosis. Values from 2.0 upward suggest meaningful insulin resistance and warrant clinical review.
homaIr.disclaimer: This calculator is for information only. If your values look elevated, talk to a clinician. Pregnancy, acute illness and some medications can distort glucose and insulin readings.

bandDescription.normal: Insulin sensitivity in the healthy range.
bandDescription.mild: Early signs of reduced insulin sensitivity — review lifestyle.
bandDescription.resistance: Meaningful insulin resistance — clinical review recommended.
bandDescription.severe: Markedly elevated — high risk for type 2 diabetes.

faq.a: HOMA-IR (Homeostatic Model Assessment for Insulin Resistance) is a simple marker of insulin resistance based on fasting plasma glucose and fasting insulin. It was introduced by Matthews et al. in 1985 and is now in worldwide clinical use.
The classic formula is HOMA-IR = (fasting insulin in µU/mL × fasting glucose in mg/dL) / 405. In SI units it is HOMA-IR = (insulin µU/mL × glucose mmol/L) / 22.5.

faq.q: What HOMA-IR values are considered normal?
faq.a: Values below 1.5 are considered normal. 1.5 to 1.99 suggests mild insulin resistance, 2.0 to 2.89 indicates insulin resistance and ≥ 2.9 is severe. Exact cut-offs vary by lab and population.
HOMA-IR is helpful when there is suspicion of insulin resistance — for example in PCOS, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, obesity or prediabetes. It supports an early estimate of diabetes risk.
Acute illness, stress, glucocorticoids, recent meals, pregnancy and some medications can shift insulin or glucose. The test requires an 8–12 hour fast.

faq.q: What should I do about a high HOMA-IR?
faq.a: An elevated value is a warning sign, not a diagnosis. Weight loss, regular physical activity and a carbohydrate-aware diet measurably improve insulin sensitivity. Discuss next steps such as HbA1c or an oral glucose tolerance test with your physician.
